description: Actuarial Statistics provides a grounding in mathematical and statistical techniques that are of particular relevance to actuarial work. Canonical representation of the official IFoA CS1 Syllabus for the 2026 Examinations (April 2025).

metadata.source: IFoA CS1 Syllabus for the 2026 Examinations
metadata.assessment: CS1A 3h20m Word; CS1B 1h50m Word and R; same sitting required

sections.title: Data analysis
sections.description: Production of simple visualisations and statistics from a data set.

topics.title: Describe the purpose and function of data analysis
topics.description: Describe the purpose and function of data analysis

learning_objectives.description: Aims of a data analysis (e.g. descriptive, inferential and predictive)
Stages and suitable tools used to conduct a data analysis to solve real-world problems
Sources of data and their characteristics, including extremely large data sets
Meaning and value of reproducible research and the elements required to ensure a data analysis is reproducible
Appropriate tools to calculate suitable summary statistics and undertake exploratory data visualisations
Interpret and make statistical inferences using Pearson’s, Spearman’s and Kendall’s measures of correlation for bivariate data
Principal component analysis to reduce the dimensionality of a complex data set

sections.title: Random variables and distributions
sections.description: The basic properties and uses of commonly-used probability distributions and the statistical properties of data generated by randomly sampling from a known distribution.

topics.title: Understand the characteristics of basic univariate distributions and how to generate samples from them
topics.description: Understand the characteristics of basic univariate distributions and how to generate samples from them

learning_objectives.description: Geometric, binomial, negative binomial, hypergeometric, Poisson and uniform discrete distributions on a finite set
Normal, lognormal, exponential, gamma, chi-square, t, F, beta and uniform continuous distributions on an interval
Evaluation of probabilities and quantiles associated with these distributions (by calculation or using statistical software as appropriate)
Poisson process and the connection between the Poisson process and the Poisson distribution
Generation of basic discrete and continuous random variables using the inverse transform method
Generation of discrete and continuous random variables using statistical software

topics.title: Determine the characteristics of jointly distributed random variables
topics.description: Determine the characteristics of jointly distributed random variables

learning_objectives.description: Probability function or density function for marginal and conditional distributions of jointly distributed random variables
The conditions under which random variables are independent
Covariance, the correlation and the expected value of a function of two jointly distributed random variables
Mean and variance of linear combinations of random variables
Conditional expectation of one random variable given the value of another random variable
Mean and variance of a random variable as an expectation of conditional expected values
Moment and cumulant generating functions of a random variable
Moment calculation via series expansion or differentiation of a generating function

topics.title: State and apply the central limit theorem
topics.description: State and apply the central limit theorem

learning_objectives.description: Central limit theorem for a sequence of independent, identically distributed random variables
Comparison of simulated samples from a given distribution with the Normal distribution

topics.title: Describe random sampling and the sampling distributions of statistics commonly used in statistical inference
topics.description: Describe random sampling and the sampling distributions of statistics commonly used in statistical inference

learning_objectives.description: Random samples from a population
The sampling distribution of a statistic
The mean and variance of a sample mean and the mean of a sample variance in terms of the population mean, variance and sample size
Basic sampling distributions for the sample mean and variance for random samples from a normal distribution
The distribution of the t-statistic for random samples from a normal distribution
The F distribution for the ratio of two sample variances from independent samples taken from normal distributions

sections.title: Statistical inference
sections.description: Use of statistics to make inferences about the process underlying a data set.

topics.title: Construct estimators and discuss their properties
topics.description: Construct estimators and discuss their properties

learning_objectives.description: Method of moments for constructing estimators of population parameters
Method of maximum likelihood for constructing estimators of population parameters
Efficiency, bias, consistency and mean square error of an estimator
Comparison of estimators using their mean square error and bias or unbiasedness
Asymptotic distribution of maximum likelihood estimators
Bootstrap method for estimating properties of an estimator

topics.title: Calculate confidence intervals and prediction intervals
topics.description: Calculate confidence intervals and prediction intervals

learning_objectives.description: Confidence interval for an unknown parameter of a distribution based on a random sample
Prediction interval for a future observation based on a model fitted to a random sample
Confidence interval for an unknown parameter using a given sampling distribution
Confidence intervals for the mean and the variance of a normal distribution
Confidence intervals for a binomial probability and a Poisson mean, including the use of the normal approximation in both cases
Confidence intervals for two-sample situations involving the normal distribution and the binomial and Poisson distributions using the normal approximation
Confidence intervals for a difference between two means from paired data
Bootstrap method to obtain confidence intervals

topics.title: Apply the concepts of hypothesis testing and goodness of fit
topics.description: Apply the concepts of hypothesis testing and goodness of fit

learning_objectives.description: Understand the concepts of Null and alternative hypotheses, simple and composite hypotheses, type I and type II errors, sensitivity, specificity, test statistic, likelihood ratio, critical region, level of significance, probability value and power of a test
Use of basic tests for the one-sample and two-sample situations involving the normal, binomial and Poisson distributions, and apply basic tests for paired data
The permutation approach to non-parametric hypothesis tests
Chi-square test to test the hypothesis that a random sample is from a particular distribution, including cases where parameters are unknown
A contingency (or two-way) table, and use of a chi-square test to test the independence of two classification criteria

sections.title: Regression theory and applications
sections.description: Use of statistics to examine and make inferences about the relationships between two or more data sets.

topics.title: Understand and use linear regression models
topics.description: Understand and use linear regression models

learning_objectives.description: Response and explanatory variables
Simple regression model (with a single explanatory variable) and multiple linear regression model (with several explanatory variables)
Least squares estimates of the slope and intercept parameters in a simple linear regression model

Use of appropriate software to fit a linear regression model to a data set and interpret the output: Perform statistical inference on the slope parameter; Describe the use of measures of goodness of fit of a linear regression model; Use a fitted linear relationship to predict a mean response or an individual response with confidence limits; Use residuals to check the suitability and validity of a linear regression model
Measures of model fit to select an appropriate set of explanatory variables

topics.title: Understand and use generalised linear models
topics.description: Understand and use generalised linear models

learning_objectives.description: Binomial, Poisson, exponential, gamma and normal distributions as an exponential family

Mean, variance, variance function and scale parameter for a generalised linear model binomial, Poisson, exponential, gamma and normal distributions. Evaluate these quantities for the distributions in 4.2.1
The link function and the canonical link function, referring to the distributions in 4.2.1
Variables, factors taking categorical values and interaction terms.
Definition of the linear predictor, including its form for simple models, including polynomial models and models involving factors
Deviance scaled deviance and estimation of the parameters of a generalised linear model
Choice of a suitable model using an analysis of deviance and examination of the significance of the parameters
Pearson and deviance residuals and their use
Statistical tests to determine the acceptability of a fitted model: Pearson’s chi-square test and the likelihood-ratio test
Fit a generalised linear model to a data set and interpret the output

sections.title: Bayesian statistics
sections.description: Use of Bayesian statistics to update prior beliefs about a data-generating process.

topics.title: Explain fundamental concepts of Bayesian statistics and use these concepts to calculate Bayesian estimators
topics.description: Explain fundamental concepts of Bayesian statistics and use these concepts to calculate Bayesian estimators

learning_objectives.description: Use of Bayes’ theorem to calculate simple conditional probabilities
Prior distribution, posterior distribution and conjugate prior distribution
Posterior distribution for a parameter in simple cases
Use of simple loss functions to derive Bayesian estimates of parameters
Credible intervals in simple cases
Credibility premium formula and the role played by the credibility factor
Bayesian approach to credibility theory and its use for calculating credibility premiums in simple cases
Empirical Bayes approach to credibility theory and its use for deriving credibility premiums in simple cases
Understanding the differences between the two approaches (Bayes v Empirical Bayes) and the assumptions underlying each of them
